title: Chapter 27: The Holding
content: The pasta is actually good.

I stand in Rowan's kitchen with a fork looped around spaghetti that tastes like someone remembered the salt this time, and I'm trying to focus on the food, on the ordinary miracle of a man who can now cook a carbonara that won't kill us both — but my eyes keep climbing up from my plate to find him.

He's doing the same thing. Fork suspended halfway to his mouth, watching me watch him. The penthouse glows. City light through floor-to-ceiling glass, Manhattan scattered like broken jewelry below us, and the air is warm and close, sealed against the October chill outside, carrying the residual scent of garlic and olive oil and something underneath that's just him — cedar and skin and the faint trace of his cologne from this morning, degraded by the day into something more honest.
The kitchen island between us might as well not exist.
"Suki told me she knows," he says.
I set my fork down. "She saw me shimmer."

"I know. She told me." He takes a bite, chews, swallows — unhurried, as if we're not standing in the wreckage of the last pretense between us. "She also told me I'd better not hurt you, and that she knows three ways to destroy a man's reputation in the fashion industry."
A beat.
"Your friend is terrifying."
Something loosens in my chest. A laugh — genuine, startled out of me. "She is. She absolutely is."

He's smiling, but his eyes are serious. The city throws amber light across his face and I think, not for the first time, that he is unbearably beautiful in the way that beautiful men always are when they don't quite believe it. That disbelief makes it worse. Makes it more.

We finish eating standing up. The plates sit abandoned on the marble counter. We haven't moved to the couch, haven't found the pretense of a movie or music or any of the respectable architectures people build around being alone together for the first time. There's just the kitchen and the window and the distance between us that keeps shortening without either of us deciding to close it.
He reaches for my hand. His fingers are warm. Pasta sauce at the corner of his mouth I want to thumb away. I don't. Not yet.
We go to the window.

He steps behind me. His arms circle my waist, loose at first, then settling. His chin finds the hollow of my shoulder and rests there, and I can feel the whole length of him pressed against my back — his chest, his hips, the soft give of his stomach beneath the cotton shirt. His breath at my ear.

The city below is endless. A thousand windows lit gold and white, a thousand strangers living their thousand separate lives, and we're suspended above all of it in this glass box with the lights off, watching.
"I've been thinking about what you said," he murmurs against my shoulder. "That loving you might trigger the shift."
My throat tightens. "It might."
"Then I want you to know —" He pauses. I feel him breathe. "I'm not afraid of what you become. I'm afraid of you becoming it alone."
I go very still.

He turns me. His hands on my shoulders, then sliding to cup the sides of my neck, thumbs resting against the pulse points below my ears. He's looking at me. Actually looking — the way no one has looked at me in four hundred years. Like he sees the hum vibrating under my skin and isn't reaching for the door.
"If it happens," he says, "I want to be there. Not finding out after. During. So you're not alone."

The hum rises. I feel it in my sternum, a sympathetic resonance to the closeness of him, to the impossible thing he just offered. I've been held before. I've been wanted before. But no one has ever asked to be present for the thing I've spent my entire existence concealing.
His heartbeat. I can feel it through my palms where they've landed on his chest, or maybe that's my own pulse, or maybe it doesn't matter anymore because they're syncopating, finding each other.
I kiss him.
Not like the first kiss — that was question and answer. Not like the second — that was desperation. This is different. This is a door I'm opening from the inside.

He tastes like wine. The cheap red he opened while cooking, tart and a little too warm, and I drink it off his tongue like communion. His hands move from my neck down the length of my spine, pressing me closer, and the hum shivers through me. I don't push it down. I don't clamp the lid shut.
I hold it.
Like holding a note in my throat. Not fighting it. Just letting it vibrate.

He walks me backward through the dark apartment. My shoulder blades hit the bedroom doorframe and he hesitates, pulling back just enough to look at me — checking, always checking — and I pull him in again because I need him to understand that stopping is the one thing I can't survive right now.

The sheets are cool against my back when we reach the bed. Cotton, high-thread-count, sliding against my bare skin as he undresses me with the care of someone unwrapping something fragile. I'm not fragile. I'm four centuries of bone and reinvention. But his hands are so gentle that I feel ancient and new at once, like something just born and something just discovered.
And he knows.

That's the difference. It's not the touch — I've been touched. It's not the desire — I've been desired. It's that he knows about the hum thrumming in my marrow. He knows about the shift waiting in my cells. He knows and he's here anyway, his mouth on my collarbone, his hands learning the terrain of my body without flinching from the strangeness of it.

I've never let someone touch me like this without bracing. Without the constant internal vigilance — monitoring the hum, suppressing the shimmer, keeping my bones locked in their current configuration through sheer force of will. Always performing the architecture of my body even while someone else's hands moved across it. Always lying with my skin.

But his heartbeat is steady against my chest. And his breath is warm on my neck. And for the first time since I don't know when — since someone whose name I've deliberately forgotten in a century I've tried to bury — I let the vigilance go.

The hum doesn't attack. It rises, yes, climbing through my ribcage like heat, but it doesn't fight me. I'm not at war with my own body. I'm riding the frequency, letting it sing through me, and every place Rowan touches is a grounding wire. His palm on my hip. His fingers spread across the small of my back. The press of his mouth to the inside of my wrist, where the pulse is closest to the surface.
The hum is part of me. It's always been part of me. And for the first time, I'm not treating it like an invading force.

His hands feel like an answer. To a question I've been asking for centuries. The question of whether anyone could touch me and stay. Whether anyone could want the truth of my body — not the presentation, not the stable configuration I wear like a costume, but the instability underneath. The shifting. The becoming.
He wants it. Or if not exactly wants it, isn't afraid of it. Isn't afraid of me.
I arch into him and the hum surges —
And that's when it happens.

My skin shimmers. Not the faint pearlescent trace from the studio — this is the real thing, the light catching the cells as they misalign, refraction through unstable tissue. It sweeps across my chest and up my throat and I feel the bones in my face begin to shift.

My jaw widens. Just a millimeter. Just enough that the geometry of my face is wrong — wider at the angles, the soft curve hardening into something squarer. My shoulders broaden beneath his hands. I can feel the deltoids reshaping, the clavicles thickening.
I freeze.

Every ounce of the peace I'd found evaporates. Terror floods in — cold, instantaneous, total. Because this is the thing, the cardinal rule, the only law that matters: I do not shift in front of people. I do not let them see. I do not let them touch me while I'm becoming something else.
"Rowan —"
His name comes out broken. A plea. A warning. Get away, I'm trying to say. Get away from me, I'm not safe, I'm not what you think, I'm —
He doesn't pull away.

His hands come up and cup my face. Both hands, fingers spread along my jaw — the jaw that is changing under his palms right now, the bone remodeling itself in real time, and he can feel it. He can feel my skull reshaping under his fingertips.
He doesn't pull away.
"I'm here." His voice is steady. Steadier than my bones. "I'm not going anywhere. Stay with me."
His eyes. Dark in the city light, fixed on mine. Not horrified. Not fascinated in the way that scientists are fascinated. Just present. Completely, unshakeably present.
I breathe.

The shift reverses. My jaw settles back — the millimeter of expansion contracting, the hard angles softening. My shoulders narrow. The shimmer fades to nothing. The hum recedes to a low throb, like a tide pulling back from shore.
I'm still me. Mostly.

My hands are shaking. I'm gripping his wrists — the wrists attached to the hands still holding my face — and I'm shaking so hard my teeth nearly chatter, and he pulls me against his chest and wraps around me like he's trying to absorb the tremors.
"It's okay," he says into my hair. "You're okay."

I'm not okay. I almost shifted. I almost became someone else in his arms. But I'm here, and he's here, and the hum is quiet for the first time in I don't know how long — not suppressed, not screaming against the walls I've built to contain it. Just quiet. Like it was heard.
Like it was held.
After.

The sheets are tangled around us. City light paints our skin in bands of amber and shadow — his chest rising and falling, my cheek against his sternum, his heartbeat a steady bass note under my ear. I can smell myself on him. Cedar and salt and the warm animal truth of two bodies that have learned each other in the dark.
His fingertip traces the line of my jaw. Slowly. Deliberately. The jaw that almost changed.
"What does it feel like?" he asks.
I consider the question. Four hundred years and no one has ever asked me this. No one has known enough to ask.
"Like every cell in my body is trying to decide what it wants to be," I say. "And the only thing that keeps it from deciding is me telling it no."
His finger pauses at the point of my chin. "What would happen if you said yes?"
Silence.
The hum, far below the surface, pulses once. A heartbeat. A knock from the inside.
I stare at the ceiling. The city glows. A siren rises somewhere in the distance, Dopplers away.
"I don't know," I say. "I've never said yes."

The words hang in the dark between us. And beneath them, the question I can't un-ask now that it's been spoken aloud: what would I become? What shape would my cells choose if I stopped holding them together by force? Would I still recognize the face in the mirror? Would I still recognize the mind behind it?

I've spent four hundred years saying no. Every suppression costs me — four hours of bone-deep agony, the price of forcing my body to stay in a shape it's trying to leave. I've calculated the cost ten thousand times. I've accepted it. The pain is the toll for remaining myself.

But now there's a man beside me with his hand on the jaw that almost changed, asking what would happen if I stopped fighting. And the question feels like a door. Like a door I've been leaning against with all my weight, and someone has just knocked from the other side.
What if I said yes?
I don't know.
I've never said yes.
The hum pulses again — patient, indifferent, older than language — and I lie very still in the circle of Rowan's arms and try not to think about how much I want to find out.
